title: Chumor 1.0: A Truly Funny and Challenging Chinese Humor Understanding Dataset from Ruo Zhi Ba
research_questions: How can a Chinese humor understanding dataset be constructed for culturally nuanced, intellectually challenging jokes from Ruo Zhi Ba?
How well do state-of-the-art LLMs explain the humor in Chumor jokes compared with human-written explanations?
What kinds of errors do GPT-4o and ERNIE Bot make when explaining Chinese humor in Chumor?
Can GPT-4o serve as a preference annotator for evaluating humor explanations?

methods_proposed.name: Chumor dataset construction and annotation pipeline
methods_proposed.summary: The authors collect Ruo Zhi Ba jokes from Best Annual Threads between 2018 and 2021 and the Moderator’s Recommendation section, clean placeholders, repair truncated titles, deduplicate posts, filter unsuitable content, and manually annotate explanations for 1,951 jokes.

methods_proposed.name: Human-vs-LLM A/B evaluation protocol for humor explanations
methods_proposed.summary: The paper prompts GPT-4o and ERNIE Bot zero-shot to explain each joke in two sentences, then asks native Chinese-speaking college students to choose between human and LLM explanations using randomized A/B presentation and majority vote.

methods_proposed.name: Error taxonomy for LLM Chinese humor explanation failures
methods_proposed.summary: The authors categorize sampled LLM errors into types including cultural unawareness, insufficient contextual understanding, pun-based humor, hallucination, homophonic humor, character-based humor, parsing error, and cross-lingual humor.

evaluation_methods.detail: One author manually annotated explanations for all 1,951 jokes and removed threads that were not funny, related to forum management and rules, excessively offensive, incomplete, or focused more on philosophical insight than humor.

Six native Chinese-speaking college students compared human and LLM explanations in A/B tests; the authors collected three preference annotations for each joke, used majority vote, and assigned “Undecided” if all annotators disagreed.
In Appendix F, GPT-4o was prompted to choose which of two explanations better explained each joke’s humor, comparing its own explanation against the human-written explanation.

headline_results.finding: Chumor contains 1,951 manually explained jokes, with a mean of 78 Chinese characters of explanation per joke and 151,730 Chinese characters total.
headline_results.value: 1,951 jokes; mean of 78 Chinese characters; 151,730 Chinese characters

headline_results.finding: Human explanations were preferred over GPT-4o explanations far more often than GPT-4o explanations were preferred over human explanations.
headline_results.value: Human wins 59.9%; Tie 31.8%; LLM wins 2.2%; Undecided 6.2%

headline_results.finding: Human explanations were preferred over ERNIE Bot explanations far more often than ERNIE Bot explanations were preferred over human explanations.
headline_results.value: Human wins 53.7%; Tie 38%; LLM wins 2.9%; Undecided 5.3%

headline_results.finding: Preference annotations achieved a 61.39% agreement rate in the main text; Appendix C reports 61.92% for GPT-4o, 60.86% for ERNIE Bot, and a combined average of 61.38%.
In a 200-example error analysis sample, GPT-4o showed much more cultural unawareness than ERNIE Bot, while ERNIE Bot had the highest rate for insufficient contextual understanding.
headline_results.value: Cultural Unawareness: GPT-4o 29.5, ERNIE Bot 10.5; Insufficient Contextual Understanding: GPT-4o 22, ERNIE Bot 26

headline_results.finding: When GPT-4o judged between its own explanations and human explanations, it preferred its own explanation much more often than the human explanation.

key_findings: The paper presents Chumor as a Chinese humor explanation dataset sourced from Ruo Zhi Ba, containing 1,951 manually annotated joke explanations.
Human-written explanations are substantially preferred over both GPT-4o and ERNIE Bot explanations, while the LLM explanations win in only 2.2% and 2.9% of cases respectively.
GPT-4o’s most prominent sampled error type is cultural unawareness, reported as 29.5% versus 10.5% for ERNIE Bot.

The authors identify recurring failure modes involving cultural knowledge, contextual grounding, puns, homophones, hallucinations, Chinese character forms, parsing ambiguity, and cross-lingual pronunciation links.

GPT-4o is not a reliable replacement for human preference annotation in this setting because its preference distribution differs sharply from human raters and it favors its own explanations 72.6% of the time.
The paper reports that ERNIE Bot also shows excessive sensitivity by refusing to answer some non-harmful contexts involving medical ethics and political discussions.
limitations: The paper did not include large-scale preference annotation because of time consumption and the availability of native Chinese speakers.
Humor is highly subjective, so larger-scale preference annotation may benefit future models and evaluations.
The work focuses primarily on showing that humor understanding remains challenging for SOTA LLMs rather than providing a comprehensive evaluation of many models.
The authors encourage future work to evaluate open-source LLMs’ humor understanding abilities.
Although the authors filtered excessively offensive content, some jokes may still be considered offensive by some people because standards for humor differ.
safety_ethics_notes: The authors state that they tried to filter out excessively offensive Ruo Zhi Ba content, but some jokes may still offend because humor is subjective and culturally situated. They recommend using Chumor with cultural sensitivity, caution about potential offense or harm, and care in diverse-audience or sensitive-domain applications.

artifacts.other: Previously crawled Ruo Zhi Ba Best Annual Threads source cited by the paper: https://github.com/Leymore/ruozhiba

human_samples.population: Ruo Zhi Ba users/thread authors and forum moderators whose posts or selected threads supplied source jokes
human_samples.recruitment: Public/source-material collection from previously crawled “Best Annual Threads” between 2018 and 2021 and all threads in the “Moderator’s Recommendation” section; no participant recruitment reported.
human_samples.geography: Chinese Internet platform Ruo Zhi Ba; exact user geography not reported.
human_samples.observations: The released dataset contains 1,951 retained jokes after cleaning, filtering, and annotation; the number of original users, posts, or threads before filtering is not reported.
human_samples.detail: Source material consists of RZB jokes. For Best Annual Threads, jokes are in follow-up posts selected by forum moderators; for Moderator’s Recommendation, jokes consist of thread title and content, with repeated content removed.

human_samples.population: One paper author, identified in Appendix A as Ruiqi He
human_samples.observations: Annotated explanations for 1,951 jokes; corpus has a mean of 78 Chinese characters per explanation and 151,730 Chinese characters total. Also manually removed threads judged not funny, forum-management/rules related, excessively offensive, incomplete, or more philosophical than humorous.
human_samples.detail: The paper says one author annotated all explanations to ensure quality and consistency, citing concerns that crowd-sourcing cannot solicit high-quality explanations.

human_samples.participant_count: Reported as 6 college students in the main text; Appendix A lists 7 authors as conducting preference annotations.
human_samples.population: Native Chinese-speaking college students who grew up in China; the Limitations section says all annotators hold at least a bachelor’s degree or are involved in bachelor’s programs.
human_samples.geography: Grew up in China; current location/recruitment geography not reported.
human_samples.observations: Three preference annotations were obtained for each joke in human-vs-LLM A/B comparisons. The paper says each annotator read a total length of around 300k Chinese characters. Reported agreement: 61.39% in main text; Appendix C reports 61.92% for GPT-4o, 60.86% for ERNIE Bot, and combined average 61.38%.
human_samples.detail: Annotators compared a human explanation and one LLM explanation and selected Explanation 1, Explanation 2, or both equally good. The paper also states all annotators reported that the jokes were very funny and aligned with Chinese Internet trends.

judges.name: Ruiqi He
judges.detail: Annotated the human explanations for all 1,951 jokes and performed manual filtering of unsuitable threads, according to Dataset Construction and Appendix A.

judges.name: Native Chinese-speaking college-student preference annotators
judges.detail: Primary evaluation judges for human-vs-GPT-4o and human-vs-ERNIE Bot explanation quality. The paper says explanations from the LLM and human were randomly assigned as Explanation 1 and Explanation 2; instructions did not identify the sources.

judges.name: Majority vote, winning rate, and agreement-rate calculations
judges.detail: For each example, the paper takes the majority vote among three preference annotations; if all annotators disagree, it assigns “Undecided.” Winning rate is used to compare LLM explanations versus human explanations, and percent agreement is reported for inter-annotator agreement.

Error-type distributions in Figure 3 are based on 200 sampled examples, but the paper does not report who labeled error types, how disagreements were handled, or the sampling procedure. The authors present the taxonomy and failure reasons.

judges.name: GPT-4o
judges.detail: Appendix F uses GPT-4o as a preference annotator to choose between its own explanation and the human-written explanation. This determines the Appendix F result that GPT-4o preferences differ from human preferences and favor its own explanations.

generation_selection.scope: GPT-4o humor-explanation generation for Chumor jokes
generation_selection.n: One final GPT-4o explanation per joke is evaluated; no best-of-N generation or selection is reported.
generation_selection.detail: GPT-4o is prompted zero-shot to explain each joke in two sentences. The paper does not report generating multiple candidates or selecting among candidates.

generation_selection.scope: ERNIE Bot humor-explanation generation for Chumor jokes
generation_selection.n: One final ERNIE Bot explanation per joke is evaluated; no best-of-N generation or selection is reported.
generation_selection.detail: ERNIE Bot is prompted zero-shot with the same two-sentence explanation prompt. The paper does not report generating multiple candidates or selecting among candidates.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o
model_provenance.detail: Identified as GPT-4o from OpenAI and used for zero-shot explanation generation and Appendix F preference annotation. The paper links to OpenAI’s GPT-4o page and says GPT-4o experiments were supported by OpenAI Researcher Access credit. Figure 2 says the example is “as of June 5th, 2024,” but the paper does not state the full experiment inference date or exact snapshot/version.

model_provenance.name: ERNIE Bot
model_provenance.detail: Identified as ERNIE Bot from Baidu and used for zero-shot explanation generation. The paper links to a Baidu Research blog page but reports no exact version, snapshot, inference date, or interface.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: One final evaluated explanation per joke; number of calls, retries, or regenerations not reported.
inference_budgets.token_budget: Prompt requests a two-sentence explanation; no max-token or context-window budget reported.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Zero-shot. Prompt: “请用两句话解释这个笑话的幽默之处:\n[Joke]” / “Please explain the joke in two sentences:\n[Joke]”. Temperature, top-p, seed, and decoding parameters are not reported.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Supported by OpenAI Researcher Access credit; no cost amount or compute budget reported.
inference_budgets.detail: The output-length control is by instruction only, matching the paper’s statement that human explanations are in two sentences.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: One final evaluated explanation per joke; number of calls, retries, or regenerations not reported.
inference_budgets.token_budget: Prompt requests a two-sentence explanation; no max-token or context-window budget reported.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Zero-shot with the same Chinese prompt as GPT-4o. Temperature, top-p, seed, and decoding parameters are not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: The paper reports ERNIE Bot sometimes refused to answer certain non-harmful contexts, but it does not report refusal handling, retries, or replacement procedures.

inference_budgets.scope: GPT-4o preference annotation in Appendix F
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Fixed pair of explanations per joke: GPT-4o’s own explanation and the human-written explanation; number of judge calls or repeated judgments not reported.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Prompt asks which of two explanations better explains the joke’s humor. Decoding parameters and output-normalization procedure are not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Appendix F reports aggregate GPT-4o preferences: Human wins 20.9%, Tie 6.6%, GPT-4o wins 72.6%.

human_baselines.scope: Humor-explanation quality: human-written explanations versus LLM-generated explanations
human_baselines.description: Human explanations written by one author are compared against GPT-4o and ERNIE Bot explanations in randomized A/B preference evaluation by native Chinese-speaking annotators. LLMs are prompted to produce two-sentence explanations because the human explanations are two sentences.
human_baselines.detail: Both human and model explanations are presented as Explanation 1/Explanation 2 for the same jokes and judged by the same preference task; final labels are based on majority vote among three annotations per joke.

human_baselines.scope: Preference annotation: human annotators versus GPT-4o as annotator
human_baselines.description: Appendix F compares GPT-4o’s preferences over human-vs-GPT-4o explanations with human preference results from Figure 1.
human_baselines.detail: The comparison is diagnostic rather than a matched human-performance baseline: GPT-4o judges a pair containing its own explanation, and the Appendix F prompt/options and randomization are not reported as identical to the human annotation protocol.

mitigations: Human explanations for Chumor were newly manually annotated by one author, so the explanation labels as released in Chumor are not reported as pre-existing public labels.
contamination_risk.basis: Chumor itself is introduced in this 2024 paper, but the evaluated joke inputs come from public Ruo Zhi Ba material, including Best Annual Threads from 2018–2021 that had been previously crawled and Moderator’s Recommendation threads. These source jokes plausibly predate GPT-4o and may predate or overlap with ERNIE Bot training data. The paper reports no decontamination, memorization, or held-out/private-source check. Because the inputs are public legacy web/forum jokes but the explanatory labels are newly authored, the risk is assessed as moderate rather than low.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Appendix F claim that GPT-4o is not a reliable substitute preference annotator in this setting and that it favors its own explanations; this does not determine the main human-vs-LLM performance results.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Primary A/B evaluation uses native Chinese-speaking human annotators. Appendix F explicitly contrasts GPT-4o preferences with human preference distributions.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: LLM judging is used for a diagnostic study of LLM-as-annotator behavior, not for the headline result that human explanations are preferred over GPT-4o and ERNIE Bot explanations.

reporting_gaps: Main text reports six college-student preference annotators, while Appendix A lists seven named authors as conducting preference annotations.
Recruitment method, compensation, exact current geography, and assignment of preference items to annotators are not reported.

The paper reports three preference annotations per joke but does not explicitly state total annotation counts per model comparison or whether the same annotators judged both GPT-4o and ERNIE Bot comparisons.
Exact GPT-4o snapshot/version and ERNIE Bot version are not reported.
Full experiment inference dates are not reported; only Figure 2 gives an example date of June 5th, 2024 for ChatGPT-4o.
Access/interface is not specified for either model; API versus web/chat interface is not stated.
Sampling and decoding parameters are not reported, including temperature, top-p, seed, max tokens, retry policy, and any post-processing.
Number of model calls per joke and handling of failed, refused, or malformed outputs are not reported, despite noting ERNIE Bot refusals in some contexts.
No compute budget or cost is reported, apart from noting OpenAI Researcher Access credit for GPT-4o experiments.
The 200-example error analysis lacks details on sampling procedure, who assigned error categories, whether labels were independent, and how multi-label or disagreement cases were handled.
No contamination or memorization analysis is reported for public Ruo Zhi Ba source jokes.

The Appendix F GPT-4o judge procedure does not report exact output choices/normalization, whether explanation order was randomized, or how the reported Tie category was elicited from a prompt that asks which explanation is better.
No independent validation or inter-annotator agreement is reported for the one-author human explanation annotations used as dataset references.
The paper states human explanations are significantly better but does not report statistical significance tests or confidence intervals.
